Venture • Airlock

At the primary airlock, the scene is one of disciplined, militaristic chaos. A massive automated cargo sled hums as it drops off two crates of heavy-duty MREs and a secure container of dark, bitter caf. Surrounding the sled are Arno, Kella, Tor, and three other Skirata warriors in deep-crimson, sand-gold, and a dull, battle-scarred blue armor are methodically checking the status of their high-yield scatter-blasters and heavy vibro-blades.

Wes Del-Fin (<@352982222636187659>) Iyola Kett (<@626586611500711967>)

Varda’s second sight does not simply read Nadia’s surface emotions; it penetrates her spiritual landscape like a lantern revealing a complex, hand-woven tapestry. Varda sees Nadia’s soul as a warp and weft of contrasting threads—the deep blue of her Mandalorian pride, the soft, pale light of her early Jedi training. But the most recent section of this weave is violently shredded and torn, the fibers charred by fire and frayed by abuse; this is the raw, agonizing trauma of her recent captivity under Rask, dominating her immediate state. Just behind these ruined threads, however, Varda detects an unnaturally smooth, clean gap—a section where the weave has been carefully and cleanly sheared away by an external blade, leaving an empty void in her history. But deepest of all, buried far beneath the charred ruins and the empty void, Varda finds a dense knot of dark, iron-hard threads. It is bound so tightly into the very core of her soul that it feels less like a pattern and more like an immovable anchor. It remains quiet, hidden under the frayed, bleeding threads of her recent suffering, but if Varda’s mind even lightly touches this deep knot, it hums with a tense, warning vibration, suggesting that any attempt to unpick it would unravel the entire piece.


The heavy door slides open with a pneumatic sigh. 

An older woman of refined, timeless grace steps into the suite. Her soft grey hair isstyled in an elegant wave that frames a face of quiet intelligence. She wears flowing, detailed robes—a deep teal under-gown draped in a rich, burgundy cloak that wraps around her shoulders like a senatorial shawl. In one hand, she holds a simple, polished wooden staff, her bearing reflecting the educated, aristocratic pedigree of the Core Worlds rather than the utilitarian grime of the Outer Rim.

A silent, armored guard follows in her shadow, carrying a simple metal tray that holds a steaming, dark clay pot of tea and two cups.

Sindara stops a few paces inside the room, her grey-toned eyes studying the two occupants. Her gaze lingers on Varda’s upright posture and the gnarled staff in her hands, her expression one of polite, guarded curiosity. She gestures to the guard to set the tray on the low table between the cots. **"The air is cold on this deck,"** she says, her voice a calm, commanding rasp that carries the educated accent of one of the Core worlds. **"I find a pot of tea is more effective than standard environmental controls for settling the chill. I hope you'll join me."**

Nadia remains seated on the edge of the bed, her visorless face setting into a wary, protective mask as she looks from Sindara to the silent guard. She doesn't reach for the tea, her manacled wrists clicking softly as she shifts her weight, her blue eyes dark with an unblinking focus as she awaits Varda's lead.

Iyola stops for a moment to take in the scene around her.  The Mandalorian warriors, all in their battered, well-used armor, were preparing for conflict.  *What a way to live,* mused Iyola.  *Win a battle, and the reward is more fighting. *
**
**“Hi everyone,”** she says.  “**Thanks for joining us for the trip. Looks like you’re preparing for a fight. She’s never seen the other three before.

She spots the container of caf and goes over to grab it.

Varda’s experienced gaze traces the lines of Sindara’s posture, reading the body language of a woman accustomed to the quiet authority of a diplomat rather than the aggressive readiness of a warlord. She holds her staff not as a weapon, but as a symbol of station, her back straight and her movements deliberate. Through the Force, her spirit is a highly organized, tightly woven tapestry of refined colors. Yet, beneath the pristine pattern of her conviction, Varda’s senses detect a dense patch of dark, grey threads of mourning and a hard, unyielding knot of resentment—the unhealed scar of a past betrayal.

Varda’s eyes drift slowly toward the steaming pot as though the offer of tea is the single most important event to occur all day. **"Ahhh,"** she sighs warmly, **"Finally. Someone on this ship with proper priorities."**

She shuffles forward a step, pale eyes flicking over the tray with exaggerated appreciation. **"You would be amazed how often people forget basic hospitality after dragging guests screaming from their ship with a grav-lock."**

The words are delivered with perfect grandmotherly pleasantness. Varda peers up at Sindara over the bridge of her nose. **"Though perhaps manners are different in the Core these days. I am terribly old-fashioned."**

Her gaze drifts lazily toward the silent armored guard looming nearby. **"When I was younger, if one abducted elderly women and their overprotective granddaughters, one at least had the manners to say why."**

A small disappointed click of her tongue follows. **"The galaxy declines in such unfortunate little ways."**

Despite herself, she smiles faintly then, as though amused by her own complaint. Slowly she lowers herself into the nearby seat beside the tea tray, setting her crooked staff across her lap. Her fingers hover near the clay pot appreciatively but do not yet pour.

**"And thank you for the tea, dear,"** she says, voice softening into something almost genuinely kind. **"Cold settles terribly into old bones once one has been tractored beamed out of the sky. I'm sure you understand. Us old women need to stick together."**

Her pale eyes lift again to Sindara’s face, deceptively hazy and grandmotherly. Yet behind them sits the unmistakable sharpness of someone who has spent a century surviving dangerous rooms. **"But I admit,"** she continues mildly, **"I am curious. One does not usually place armed guards outside the doors of travellers."**
**"So now that you have deigned us with your presence, a dated if reliable interrogation tactic to be sure, would you kindly mind explaining why you are keeping us against our will?"**

Wes entered the primary airlock area alongside Iyola.  He felt a subtle shift in her as they looked upon Arno and the others.  What he saw was a well organized squad taking inventory and checking weapons.   A small smile appeared as he remembered similar times back during the Clone War.   Prepping for a mission and the chatter between squad mates.   He was only a teenager then but the memories were fond ones.  


He nodded in greeting to Arno and the others as Iyola spoke, giving a quick rundown of what they were expecting.  Mainly no hostilities.  He expanded on what she had said as she moved on the caf like a mynoc on some power cables.   **”Both sides have something the other wants.  They won’t admit it but they also need more help.”**

Sindara listens to Varda’s calculated, sharp grumbling with a faint, appreciative curve of her lips. She does not appear offended by the barbed indignation. **"You have my sincere apologies for the... less than graceful reception,"** she says, her voice smooth and refined. **"I am Captain Sindara, representing the Cooperative. I assure you, our hospitality was not intended as a permanent confinement."**

She turns her head slightly toward the silent, charcoal-clad guard standing near the door, giving a short, decisive nod. **"Release her."**

The guard steps forward, his heavy gauntlets pulling a sleek data-key from his belt. He taps a code into Nadia’s wrist-binders; with a soft, pressurized *click-hiss*, the heavy metallic cuffs release, sliding from her gauntlets and dropping onto the diagnostic bed. Nadia rub her wrists, her blue eyes narrowing in surprise, though she keeps her distance.

Sindara sits in the chair opposite Varda, her staff resting elegantly against her knee as she watches the two women. **"The tractor beam was a defensive precaution, not an act of malice."** 

She reaches out, pouring a single cup of the steaming, dark tea and sliding it gently across the table toward Varda. **"However, the situation has resolved itself. Approximately twenty minutes ago, we received a secure transmission from Bespin. Your Captain, Iyola Kett, has established contact with our bridge. She has one of ours, Elara Vance, safe and intact."** She looks from Varda to Nadia. **"We are currently bound for the Hesperis Nebula. We are heading to an ancient, decommissioned relay station to facilitate a safe, neutral exchange of personnel. You are going home."**

Varda takes the steaming cup, looking into the steam as it rises into the air. Then she grumbles and she tilts her lips with an appreciative smile. **"It's always maddening when your kidnappers are the sensible sort. Much harder to hold a grudge."** She brings the tea to her lips and takes a deep sip. 

Then she tilts her head to the side and she smiles. **"Now I don't claim to know much about this exchange point. But did you truly have to tempt the spirits by having the hand over in a most excellently mysterious sounding compound. You're practically inviting mischief. These things have power you know. Names, places these things matter."** She takes another sip of the tea then shakes her head and she looks back forward. **"Or I am a paranoid old lady. Alas it can't be helped."**

She puts the porcelain down. **"You have me at a loss i must confess. The cooperative is a delightfully sophisticated name, it evokes many things. But yet i fear i have no idea what that is. Would you care to enlighten us."** 

She looks back round to Nadia. **"We don't have much better to do. so were all ears open on the matter."**

Sindara lets out a low, polite chuckle at Varda’s warning about "tempting the spirits," her Core-world skepticism clear, though she respects the elder’s passion. **"I am afraid we chose the relay station for its sensor-blocking properties, not for its mystical resonance, Elder Nisyren. If there is mischief waiting for us there, it will be of the mundane variety."**

She sits back, her posture relaxing slightly. **"The Cooperative is, at its heart, an alliance of exiles, scholars, and those who grew tired of watching the past be stored in jars. We are the counter-force to the people who put you in those stasis tubes—an organization we know as the Protector Order."**

She gestures toward the table. **"The Protectors believe that history is a resource to be controlled, and that key individuals—Sleeper assets like yourself—must be kept on ice until a date they deem significant. We believe that is a theft. Your life belongs to you, not to a secret order's grand design. The Cooperative exists to locate these hidden caches, break the locks, and return your autonomy. We provide Safe Harbors—Sanctuaries—where you can recover and choose your own path. We do not demand your service. We only offer choice."**

Nadia’s gaze drifts away from the table as Sindara speaks. Her blue eyes go glass-like and unblinking, fixed on the distant, silent movement of the hyperdrive’s blue energy passing outside the observation port. She does not look at Varda or Sindara, her posture going completely still, her breath slow and shallow. It is not a violent fit, but a sudden, total dissociation.

The silent Mandalorian in the grey plate offers a sharp, physical nod. He taps the stock of his carbine, his posture a quiet, unyielding promise of violence.

Sindara remains motionless in her seat, her elegant, grey-streaked brow furrowing slightly as she watches the older woman work. Her gaze is sharp, analytical—she does not miss the subtle, intentional twitch of Varda's finger, nor the way the steam from the tea seemed to bend almost unnaturally toward the younger woman. She stays silent, observing with a guarded, academic interest, realizing the "frail old grandmother" is far more capable than her performance suggests.

At first, Nadia does not react. Her blue eyes remain fixed on the viewport, her face pale and still. But as Varda's Force presence gently wraps around her, carrying the scent of dry sage and the physical warmth of her fingers, the heavy, grey mist in her mind begins to lift. 

She blinks once, twice, the vacant glaze in her eyes suddenly shattering as her focus returns to the room. She draws a sudden, sharp breath, her head jerking slightly as if emerging from deep water. She looks down at Varda’s hand on her arm, then at the bundle of green leaves, her brow furrowing in confusion. 

She looks at Sindara, then back to Varda, her posture slowly returning to its guarded, protective line, though she looks physically drained. **"I'm sorry… you were saying?"**

The heavy door slides closed with a final, pressurized click, the sound of the secure locks engaging leaving the suite once more in a quiet, isolated stillness. Nadia stands from the edge of the diagnostic bed, her gloved fingers gently rubbing her wrists where the manacles had left deep, red rings of pressure. The frantic, jittery energy that had dominated her has vanished, replaced by a quiet, physical exhaustion.

She looks down at the low wooden table where the cooling tea remains. **"Thank you, Varda,"** she says, her voice a soft, raw rasp that lacks any of her previous warrior's defiance. **"I don't... I don't know what happened to me just then. It felt like I was drowning in my own thoughts."**

She walks back toward the viewport, her scuffed boots making a soft, silent slide on the floor. She peers out at the rushing cobalt tunnel of hyperspace, her blue eyes fixed on the distant, swirling patterns.

Two hours in the shifting, cobalt-blue tunnel of hyperspace passes in a low, vibrating hum that resonates through the *Venture*’s structural ribs. Throughout the transit, the ship becomes a closed loop of focused preparation. The initial disorientation of the newly awakened begins to fade, replaced by the practical, demanding routines of survival in an unfamiliar era.

Down in the warmth of the lower tier, the engineering bay is alive with the rhythmic clink of metal and the low-frequency thrum of the primary power grid. Under Lis's watchful, diagnostic visor, Misha works with a feverish, grateful focus. She is the hands, and he is the mind. Working together, they systematically dismantle the first of the recreation room’s powered-down simulation pods, isolating its high-throughput environmental stabilizer. Guided by Lis’s precise High Republic calculations, Misha uses her heavy quantum scanner to route a three-phase circuit from the stabilizer directly into the primary weapons bus.

**"The constant-load bypass is holding, Master Garah,"** Misha says, her voice small but bright with a rare, victorious energy as she wipes grease from her visor. She gestures to the secondary terminal where the diagnostic lines have finally smoothed into a steady, glowing blue. **"We’ve successfully shunted the excess charge through the cargo hold’s heavy-duty heaters. We don't have a full offensive array, but the port-side point-defense turret is officially online and drawing a steady current. If they try to box us in, we can actually bite back."**

In the quiet sanctuary of the recreation room, the massive holoscreen continues to cycle its animated alien forest, but the serene scene is ignored. Ciara remains in her corner, her magenta skin painted in the cool, blue light of her datapad terminal as she devours the records. The historical accounts of the Betrayal of the Jedi following the end of the Clone Wars, the account of their attempted Coup, foiled only by the Clone forces and the systematic eradication of her Order confirm her instincts; the silence she felt in the Force wasn't a localized blockage—it was a graveyard. 

Elara Vance sits nearby, her long-empty tea cup resting on the table. She has remained respectful of the Jedi’s silence, but her gaze occasionally drifts to the corridor, her posture growing more rigid as the clock ticks toward the end of the transit. 

In the secondary barracks, Arno’s squad maintains their vigil. The smooth, synthesized voice of the AI suddenly echoes throughout the ship’s compartments, preceded by a soft, priority chime. ***"Reversion from hyperspace in thirty standard seconds. All hands, please prepare for transition to real-space. Captain Kett, please report to the bridge. We are entering the Hesperis Nebula."***

The cobalt tunnel outside the panoramic viewports begins to ripple and stretch, the lines of infinite light snapping back into individual, distant suns. The *Venture* lurches—a sharp, structural groan as its hyperdrive disengages—and the ship drops into the thick, bruised-purple haze of the Hesperis nebula. 

Looming out of the deep violet mists, silent and massive, is **Relay Station 7-Delta**. A relic of the High Republic's "Great Works," its skeletal, grey structure looks like a dormant metal spider floating in the dust. Its docking arms are cold, dark, and unpowered, yet at the primary ventral ring, a single, glowing light indicates that the automated systems have successfully cycled in response to the *Venture*'s handshake protocol.

This was the second time Wes had mentioned a bond.  Maybe it was some Jedi philosophical terminology.  “**What’s the difference between a bond and an attachment?**” she asked.  “**Isn’t love always a bond? That’s why it’s love.”  **

The mention of Ghurn is puzzling.**  “Sorry if I’m missing something, but what did Ghurn tell you?”** She gives the tiniest suggestion of a shrug at Wes’s suggestion.  **“Sometimes I think that I understand less the longer I live this new life.  But sure.  Maybe you’ll hear my story one day.”  ** 

She was about to check on the time to the base when Eve interjected.  **“Let’s go to the bridge,”** said Iyola, after hearing out anything else Wes had to add.  “**Time to meet Sindara and get back Nadia and Varda.”**

Iyola’s question about the bond was a good one.  She was perceptive.  That was good and a reason he chose for her to be Captain.   He nodded and smiled as he remembered.  **”Love is a bond.  That is a fact.  This was something more.   It involved the Force.   There was a,”**  He paused searching for the right word.  His head nodded slightly as it came to him.  **”A sense of knowing, sensing the others presence form a short or even a great distance.  We knew if the other was safe and well.  Communication without words.  There was also a physical manifestation.  A sense of Force energy when we touched.   I’ve inquired when I could about that but never found or heard of anything like it ever happening.”**


He watched as he saw her process what he had said.  Her proclamation about understanding less as time went on had him nodding in agreement.  The chance to learn about her story was greeted with a nod and a small smile but he felt the need to explain what Ghurn had said before it sat too long.   He nodded as he mentioned heading to the bridge.  He turned and started walking.  


The smile slipped away though when he began to answer her about Ghurn.  **”It was more of what Ghurn asked that I not do.”**  He saw the questioning look in Iyola’s eyes and he answered quickly.  **”I agreed that I would not seek out Nadia’s younger sister,”**. He paused for a second.  His voice when he spoke again was softer as if saying a name he hadn't uttered in a long time.  The name of his mother.  **”Kiera.”**  As he said the name he let out the breath he had been holding.  **”My daughter.”**

Nadia watches as Varda Nisyren settles her withered frame onto the cold deck plating, her legs crossing with a slow, deliberate care that seems to defy her 102 years. Her gnarled staff lies flat across her lap, its three beskar rings cold but resonant against the fabric of her patchwork skirt.

With a slow, heavy sigh, Varda closes her eyes, pulling her consciousness away from the clinical hum of the *Corrigendum* and diving deep into the roiling currents of the Force. The constant, dull pain in her bones—the unhealed scar of her preservation—flares to a sharp, localized heat in her stomach, but with a master's will, she smooths the jagged edges of the pain until it is nothing more than a simmering, familiar ache. She reaches out through the dimension of hyperspace, her mind homing in on the unique, bright teal "spark" that is Iyola Kett. 

The future unfolds before her mind's eye not as a chronological sequence, but as a vast, shifting loom of grey mist and overlapping threads.

At the center of the vision is Ghost Station 7-Delta, appearing as a massive, skeletal silhouette of dead durasteel suspended in a bruised-purple nebula. She sees Iyola’s teal thread and Sindara’s solid, burgundy line crossing at the station’s primary docking ring. The exchange itself is clear—a necessary, logical path—but as the threads weave together, Varda’s sight catches three distinct, unaligned variables waiting in the shadows of the station:

First, she sees a silver, creeping thread—thin as a spider’s line—that does not belong to the Skiratas or the crew. It crawls silently along the hull of the *Venture* the moment the two ships dock, clinging to its obsidian plating like an invisible parasite. 

Second, the vision is assaulted by a sudden, brilliant flash of blue-white light—not the fire of an explosion, but a massive, blinding discharge of static that scrambles the sensors, leaving both ships blind and deaf.

Third, she senses a cold, heavy absence lurking deep within the lower, unpowered decks of the station itself. It is a dense, metallic pocket of energy that is entirely dormant, waiting for someone to find it.

The vision snaps, returning Varda to the quiet, chilly reality of the observation ward just as the hyperdrive’s low thrum begins to pitch upward, signaling they are nearing the end of their transit.

**"Sensor scan complete, Wes Del-Fin,"** Eve’s voice flows through the bridge console as the screen flickers with a series of low-resolution telemetry lines. **"I am registering a massive thermal signature docked on the far side of the station's primary solar arrays, consistent with the cruiser *Corrigendum*. No other active transponders are currently detected within our three-kilometer scanning limit. The local ion interference is holding at sixty-eight percent, which will continue to limit standard tactical resolution."**

The bridge doors iris open, and Kella steps through, her hand lightly resting on her holster as she guides Elara Vance and Ciara onto the command deck. **"Passenger Vance is on the bridge as requested, Captain. She kept her hands to herself, but her eyes have been all over our primary routing panels."** She nods toward Wes before stepping back to stand guard near the bridge entrance. **"My squad is staged and ready."**

Elara Vance ignores the guard's suspicion, her gaze immediately locking onto the panoramic viewport. The view of **Station 7-Delta**—its grey, web-like structure floating like a dead predator in the purple fog—causes her to draw a slow, appreciative breath. **"The *Corrigendum* is already docked,"** she says softly, her eyes tracking the distant, blue-tinged silhouette of the heavy cruiser hidden behind the station's array. **"Sindara is waiting."**

Misha Vorne enters from the secondary access shaft, her face smudged with grease but her eyes sparkling with a rare, victorious energy. She practically bounces onto the deck, gesting proudly to Lis beside her. **"Captain Kett, Wes... Master Garah has done it! The bypass is holding!"** She taps a diagnostic panel on the tactical console, bringing up a single, glowing green indicator. **"The port-side point-defense turret is officially online and drawing a steady current. If those cruisers try to box us in, we can actually bite back. It's... it's a masterpiece of un-stabilized engineering."**

Varda's face remains utterly still as the vision crashes over her. Years of practice allow her to watch rather than drown. The threads unfold, twist, and knot together before her inner eye. She studies each one with the careful attention of an old weaver examining a tapestry for flaws. **"Hm."**

The sound escapes her almost absently. Not fear, never fear, more akin to an Appreciation. Whoever had arranged this had done so with care. The silver thread creeping along the Venture's hull. The burst of static waiting to blind friend. The hidden thing sleeping beneath the station's bones.

Layer upon layer. She could respect that. Not the intent. But the craftsmanship. The vision recedes slowly, like a tide withdrawing from a shore. Ghost Station 7-Delta dissolves into mist and distant stars. The currents of possibility loosen their grip and release her back into the present. For a moment she simply breathes. Old bones. Cold air. The gentle ache in her stomach.

Reality. Varda opens her eyes. She reaches for her staff and uses it to lever herself upright with a quiet grunt.

There is no urgency in her movements. No panic. Only certainty. Yet for the briefest moment the Force around her vibrates like a plucked string. A deft turn of her wrist. A thought sharpened into a spear then hurtled through the force. like a bolt of lightning. She sends it hurtling across the currents between minds.

'Unknown.' her first thought echoes winding towards the Prescence shinning like beskar steel, A heartbeat later, 'EMP' towards the teal colour of Iyolas core.

The message is stripped bare of explanation, carrying only certainty and the unmistakable weight of Varda's conviction. Then she exhales. **"Well,"** she murmurs to nobody in particular. **"That is unfortunate."**

*The engineer is still writing down a report on Vorne's skills, continuing the 'internship', taking it seriously, his gaze raises as she praises him.* **"Mhn...? Oh yes, things will hold for now... may burn a few wires or overload some of the light fixtures but it is serviceable in a military emergency"** *He closes the report, paying attention to the matter at hands.* **"We will need to undo it at the earliest chance we have, this is not the type of fix that can be left as is... but I'm sure you do not care for the details, so I won't take more of your time, Captain"** *He stands to attention for now, what he lacks in social skill he still has (somewhat) in professionality*

Ciara perks up a bit at the announcement that they have arrived at 7-Delta. She puts aside her reading, mostly satisfied that she's got the gist of the major concerns of the era. With a deep breath, she stands and readies herself for the docking procedures. It doesn't take long for an escort to arrive for Elara, requesting their presence on the bridge. 

She muses more about the nature of her ailment as she walks. Her indifference towards the others, her lack of engagement, all unsettlingly out of character for her, but after so much of her life being dictated by her emotional whims, she finds it nearly impossible to muster motivation without them. 

She pushes the thoughts away again. The only thing she can do is continue to make an effort to act normal until things settle down, then seek out someone who can fix whatever is wrong with her. For now, she observes the room. Lots of strong feelings floating around, just out of reach. She tries not to let it distract her. 

Apart from a polite smile and a small wave as she enters, she adds nothing to the ongoing conversation. She inwardly curses herself, sure that her lack of knowledge on the topic wouldn't have prevented her from saying *something* before. Yet still, she is unable to think of anything suitable, so she allows herself to sink into the background for now.

“**Thank you, Kella,” **said Iyola.  This exchange was an exercise in trust, something she wouldn’t expect a Mandalorian to know much about . **“Elara, I’m contacting the Corrigendum now - got a message for Sindara?  Wes, any thoughts on the best way to do this handoff?”**

Everything seemed like it was going fine. It was just them and the Corrigendum.  But Iyola couldn’t help remembering the other ships hiding in that other nebula, in all the wreckage.  Not a lot of wreckage here, but -  **“Garah, Misha, awesome work on the weapons.  Any way you can help damp down some of that ion interference so we can get a better look before we meet up?”**

CIara silently joined them. If she had any thoughts on the exchange, she kept them to herself.

Wes listened as Eve ran through the sensor sweep of the area.   He nodded slightly as she finished.  There wasn’t anything out there other than the two ships and the relay station.  Iyola’s question to him about how to handle this had him look up from his station's controls and out the view port.   **”Calm and cool.  The last thing we need is a misunderstanding.”** He turned his head and looked at Elara and then to Iyola.  **”Both sides have something the other wants.  Let’s find out and get our people back.”**

On the bridge of the *Venture*, the atmosphere remains stretched to a taut wire. Wes sits motionless at the tactical console, his hands flat on the panels as he withdraws his consciousness from the cold void of the station, his searching thoughts finding only the dead, metallic silence of the Hesperis nebula. The source of the warning remains elusive to him, a ghost in the static.

The blue-tinged hologram of Captain Sindara flickers as she monitors the status updates from her own command deck. She looks at Iyola, her sharp-featured face a study in professional, cold assessment. **"We have completed our scan of the lower decks. She steps closer to the transmitter, her gaze lingering on Elara Vance's quiet, watching presence on the *Venture*'s bridge. **"The parley is moving to Docking Arm 3, now."**

Varda springs into action, the sudden speed of it at odds with her age, she's a coiled snake finally allowed to uncoil. She grasps Nadia's hand and hauls herself up, her staff striking the deck once for leverage. "**We must move. Threads unseen work in the shadows. A saboteur. The future is in motion, we must now navigate its currents. The storm is upon us, my dear. Let us now revel in its winds**" She whips her cloak over her shoulder and stands firm. "**A bug crawls along the Venture this must be our first priority.**"

Her grip on Nadia's hand lingers a beat longer than the urgency requires, steadying herself as much as steadying the girl, the vision has not fully released her yet, some part of her still standing ankle-deep in that grey, shifting mist. When her pale eyes finally focus, truly focus, there is nothing grandmotherly left in them. Only the flat, weighing patience of something that has read a hundred years of weather and then learned never to trust the calm before it.

"**Silver and thin, that thread. Patient. It does not announce itself, it waits**" She barks a laugh its gnarled and quick. "**the way all good poison waits."** She taps her staff twice against the deck, and the three beskar rings shiver with a low, resonant hum **"Whoever set this snare set three. She begins moving toward the sealed door, each step deliberate, her staff striking the deck in a slow, even rhythm not the shuffle of an old woman, but something closer to a witch winding down a threaded path. "**I have already sent word ahead. They will know the shape of it, if not the meaning. "**Come, quick hands. Let us go meet our silver little guest before it decides to introduce itself on its own terms."**

***Nothing.*** He thought to himself as he sensed nothing out of the ordinary.  With a frustrated breath he typed a quick message that appeared on the screens that only the bridge crew could see.   *Nothing out of the ordinary.*  He turned and looked at Iyola.   A quick shake of his helmeted head indicated a negative.

His current job was a huge step down from what he used to perform in the eras past... but as an engineer he trusts routine and numbers he can interpret, not leaps of faith and hoping things will work out for the best... he doesn't move and stands behind Captain Kett, arms behind his back, chest held out and chin up high: he is not abandoning his commitment to her or her ship.
